title: Your First Variable — the Labeled Box
instruction:
Think of a variable as a **labeled box**. The label is the name, and what you put inside is the value. In Go, you create a variable with `var`:

```
var name string = "Alice"
```

This creates a box labeled `name`, declares that it holds text (`string`), and puts `"Alice"` inside it.

Create a variable `name` with the value `"Alice"` and print it.

title: The Quick Way — Short Declaration
instruction:
Writing `var name string = "Alice"` every time is verbose. Inside a function, `:=` does the same thing in one step — Go figures out the type for you:

```
name := "Alice"  // Go infers this is a string
```

This is the most common way to declare variables in Go. Declare `name` as `"Alice"` and `age` as `17` using `:=`, then print both on separate lines.
hint: name := "Alice" and age := 17 on separate lines — then print each.

title: A Float for GPA
instruction:
Numbers with decimal points are `float64` in Go. Alice's GPA is 3.8 — that's a float.

You now have three facts about this student. Declare all three and print them:
- `name` = `"Alice"` (string)
- `age` = `17` (int)
- `gpa` = `3.8` (float64)

Print each on its own line.
hint: gpa := 3.8 — Go infers float64. Print all three with separate Println calls.

title: True or False — Booleans
instruction:
A `bool` holds either `true` or `false` — nothing else. It's perfect for yes/no questions: "Is this student enrolled?" 

Add a fourth variable: `enrolled := true`. Then print all four student details.
hint: enrolled := true — then fmt.Println(enrolled). It will print the word "true".

title: Zero Values — Empty Boxes
instruction:
What happens if you create a variable but don't give it a value? In Go, every variable gets a **zero value** automatically — Go never leaves a box empty:

- `int` → `0`
- `float64` → `0`
- `string` → `""` (empty string)
- `bool` → `false`

Declare a `var score int` without any value, and a `var comment string` without any value. Print both — you should see `0` and an empty line.
hint: var score int — no = needed. fmt.Println(score) will print 0. fmt.Println(comment) will print a blank line.

title: Updating a Variable
instruction:
Variables can change — that's the whole point. Alice just got her exam back: she scored `78` initially, but the teacher added `5` bonus points.

Start with `score := 78`, add 5 to it, then print the updated score.
hint: score = score + 5 — or the shorthand: score += 5.

title: Type Conversion
instruction:
Go never converts types automatically — you have to be explicit. Alice's GPA is `3.8` (float64), but the school system needs it as an integer (it truncates the decimal).

Convert `gpa` from `float64` to `int` and print the result. You should see `3`.
hint: intGpa := int(gpa) — wrapping a value with a type name converts it. Print intGpa.

title: Constants — Values That Never Change
instruction:
Some values should never change — the minimum passing grade, the maximum GPA, the school's name. Use `const` for these:

```go
const maxGPA = 4.0
```

A constant declared with `const` cannot be updated later — Go will refuse to compile if you try.

A `const` named `minPassScore` is already set to `60`. Alice scored `83`. Calculate how many points above the minimum she scored (`83 - 60 = 23`) and print the result.
hint: margin := aliceScore - minPassScore — then print margin.
